channel.title: dotnet/fsharp Issues
channel.description: Issues from https://github.com/dotnet/fsharp, generated by https://github.com/meziantou/IssuesToRss/

item.title: Issue: Deadlock occurs after updating to FSharp.Core 5.0.2 in some rare cases - @FoggyFinder
item.description:
MCVE

.fsproj

<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
  <PropertyGroup>
    <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>
    <UseWPF>true</UseWPF>
    <TargetFramework>net5.0-windows</TargetFramework>
    <DisableWinExeOutputInference>true</DisableWinExeOutputInference>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Include="Program.fs" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <PackageReference Update="FSharp.Core" Version="5.0.2" />
  </ItemGroup>
</Project>

.fs

open System
open System.Windows
open System.Windows.Controls
open System.Threading.Tasks
open System.Collections.Concurrent
open System.Threading

let callOnUiThread f = 
    // works with TaskCreationOptions.RunContinuationsAsynchronously
    let tcs = new TaskCompletionSource<bool>()
    Application.Current.Dispatcher.BeginInvoke(Action(fun () ->
        try
            f()
            let res = string <| tcs.TrySetResult(true)
            ("res = " + res) |> printfn "%A"
        with exp ->
            let res = string <| tcs.TrySetException(exp)
            ("res = " + res) |> printfn "%A"
    )) |> ignore
    tcs.Task :> Task

type SomeView() =
    let bc = new BlockingCollection<int>()
    do bc.Add(90)
    let cs = new CancellationTokenSource()

    let lb = ListBox()
    let addItems c =
        callOnUiThread (
            fun () ->
                for i in 0..c do 
                    let lbl = Label(Content = string i, Margin = Thickness 5.0)
                    lb.Items.Add(lbl) |> ignore
        )

    let loop() =
        let mutable count = 0
        let mutable iter = 0
        let mutable flag = true

        let c = async {
            do! Async.Sleep 1000
            while flag do
                if bc.TryTake(&count, 800) then
                    do! addItems count |> Async.AwaitTask
                if iter > 1000 then
                    flag <- false
                iter <- iter + 1
            bc.CompleteAdding()
        }
        
        Async.Start(c, cs.Token)

    do loop()

    member _.View = lb

    interface IDisposable with 
        member _.Dispose() = 
            cs.Cancel()
            bc.CompleteAdding()
            bc.Dispose()

let view = new SomeView()
let win = Window(Height = 500.0, Width = 540.0, Content = view.View)

[<STAThread>]
Application().Run win |> ignore

(view :> IDisposable).Dispose()

The code above works fine until PR #11142 is merged where TaskContinuationOptions.ExecuteSynchronously option is specified to a continuation.

It seems like

tcs.TrySetResult(true)

line is blocking execution until async isn't finished its job.

Not sure whether it's a bug or not.

item.title: Issue: F# as a first-class .NET language - future - @denis-dbm
item.description:
This question could be already answered, but I have not found a concrete and direct answer.

What is the future of F#? Does it remain, in long-term, as a first-class a language like C#? Did Microsoft pronounce oficially about the F# future?

I am interested in F# as an option to develop .NET applictions, suggesting it in enterprise scenarios, introducing functional programming for some teams. Do you have examples of companies that are using F# and them having a good experience and performance? I wish hear real experiences, from experienced engineers and developers: this is why I am posting here.

Thanks.

item.title: Issue: Cambridge suite has intermittent Test failures - @KevinRansom
item.description: In the CI we see failures occasionally that succeed on rerun. Obviously this is not great ... and I am looking at it.

item.title: Issue: Keep track of keyword in SynExpr.IfThenElse - @nojaf
item.description:
Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

When a SynExpr.IfThenElse is created, the used keyword at the start of the expression is not captured in the AST.

Example:

if a then b

if x then y elif z then foo else bar

if d1 then d2 else if d3 then d4 else d5

relevant AST:

[DoExpr
    (NoDebugPointAtDoBinding,
    IfThenElse
    (Ident a, Ident b, None,
        DebugPointAtBinding tmp.fsx (1,0--1,9) IsSynthetic=false, false,
        tmp.fsx (1,0--1,9) IsSynthetic=false,
        tmp.fsx (1,0--1,11) IsSynthetic=false),
    tmp.fsx (1,0--1,11) IsSynthetic=false);
DoExpr
    (NoDebugPointAtDoBinding,
    IfThenElse
    (Ident x, Ident y,
        Some
        (IfThenElse
            (Ident z, Ident foo, Some (Ident bar),
            DebugPointAtBinding
                tmp.fsx (3,12--3,23) IsSynthetic=false, false,
            tmp.fsx (3,12--3,23) IsSynthetic=false,
            tmp.fsx (3,12--3,36) IsSynthetic=false)),
        DebugPointAtBinding tmp.fsx (3,0--3,9) IsSynthetic=false, false,
        tmp.fsx (3,0--3,9) IsSynthetic=false,
        tmp.fsx (3,0--3,36) IsSynthetic=false),
    tmp.fsx (3,0--3,36) IsSynthetic=false);
DoExpr
    (NoDebugPointAtDoBinding,
    IfThenElse
    (Ident d1, Ident d2,
        Some
        (IfThenElse
            (Ident d3, Ident d4, Some (Ident d5),
            DebugPointAtBinding
                tmp.fsx (5,14--5,29) IsSynthetic=false, false,
            tmp.fsx (5,14--5,29) IsSynthetic=false,
            tmp.fsx (5,14--5,40) IsSynthetic=false)),
        DebugPointAtBinding tmp.fsx (5,0--5,10) IsSynthetic=false,
        false, tmp.fsx (5,0--5,10) IsSynthetic=false,
        tmp.fsx (5,0--5,40) IsSynthetic=false),
    tmp.fsx (5,0--5,40) IsSynthetic=false)]

It would be very interesting to know how the expression started.

Describe the solution you'd like

I'd like to store the information as followed:

[<NoEquality; NoComparison; RequireQualifiedAccess>]
type IfThenElseStart =
    | If of range: Range
    | Elif of range: Range

Describe alternatives you've considered

It is possible to retrieve this information via the F# tokens. Although, in some scenario's it can be tricky.

Additional context

This is another one of those areas that could simplify Fantomas quite a bit. As we know need to puzzle together the AST nodes with the tokens to relate them. I'm interested in submitting a PR for this if accepted.

item.title: Issue: file type .fsx should be associate with fsi.exe on windows. - @cnmade
item.description:
For linux user ,who want to run FSharp script ,they can using Shebang, like

#!/usr/bin/env -S dotnet fsi
printfn "Hello FSharper"

But for windows, it is more hard.

If the fsi.exe can handle each .fsx type. When user double click file , it will execute the script.

Current we do many script on windows with python. Double click .py file, will execute it with python.exe

If fsi support direct run .fsx file, we can replace our script with FSharp. No more python needed. That's awesome

item.title: Issue: Update the use of optional parameters in the FCS public API so that optional arguments are exposed as C#-compatible default arguments - @baronfel
item.description:
Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

As a reference, see this twitter thread with Brad Wilson of xunit.

Non-F# consumers of FCS are forced to supply every optional parameter to common entry points like FSharpChecker.Create (example), whereas in F# we do not (example).

Describe the solution you'd like

To play nice in the ecosystem we should expose these optional parameters per the mechanisms described in the F# documentation for interop with non-F# languages. This would prevent C# (and presumably VB) consumers (like xunit, nunit, sharplab, etc) from being forced to reference types like LegacyReferenceResolver that are obsolete directly, which causes warnings in their build processes.

This might be a binary-breaking change, but from an implementation perspective the default values for these parameters would be null (as it is today), and since 't option has null as the representation for None this may not even be a big deal.

Describe alternatives you've considered

  • We could not do this work, and non-F# consumers would continue to have to specify every parameter on these callsites.
  • We could provide overloads of Create (and possibly other common entrypoints) that don't have this explicit reference to the obsolete LegacyReferenceResolver type, so that non-F# consumers have a path towards using the library without touching obsolete code.

item.title: Issue: Generic parameters on struct type cannot be referenced in method constraints when no implicit constructor is defined - @DalekBaldwin
item.description:
Consider these two interfaces:

type IFoo<'T> =
    abstract Make : unit -> 'T

type IBar<'T> =
    abstract Do<'I when 'I :> IFoo<'T>> : 'I -> 'T

IBar<'T> can be implemented just fine on a class:

type Bar<'T> () =
    interface IBar<'T> with
        member __.Do<'I when 'I :> IFoo<'T>> (i:'I) =
            i.Make ()

But the same interface implementation on a struct type with no implicit constructor:

[<Struct>]
type Bar<'T> =
    interface IBar<'T> with
        member __.Do<'I when 'I :> IFoo<'T>> (i:'I) =
            i.Make ()

yields error FS0039: The type parameter 'T is not defined. at the point of the type constraint.

This affects struct records and unions as well; the following types yield the same error:

[<Struct>]
type Bar<'T> =
    { Blah : int}
    interface IBar<'T> with
        member __.Do<'I when 'I :> IFoo<'T>> (i:'I) =
            i.Make ()

[<Struct>]
type Bar<'T> =
| Blah of int
    interface IBar<'T> with
        member __.Do<'I when 'I :> IFoo<'T>> (i:'I) =
            i.Make ()

Known workarounds

The struct type compiles just fine if the compiler can fully infer the corresponding method signature, including constraints, that correspond to the interface:

[<Struct>]
type Bar<'T> =
    interface IBar<'T> with
        member __.Do i =
            i.Make ()

But this is not always possible if other type arguments need to be referenced explicitly in the method body for other purposes.

For now, on empty struct types, I'm adding implicit constructors corresponding to what the compiler usually generates when one is omitted.

[<Struct>]
type Bar<'T> (__dummy:int) = // ...

// or

[<Struct>]
type Bar<'T> (__dummy:byte) = // ...

Environment: Windows 10 Microsoft (R) F# Interactive version 12.0.0.0 for F# 5.0 .NET 6.0.0-preview.5.21301.5

item.title: Issue: Update ASP.NET Core F# project templates to use new minimal hosting APIs in .NET 6 - @KevinRansom
item.description:
F# equivalent of #33944

The ASP.NET Core project templates included in the SDK should be updated to use the new minimal hosting APIs introduced in .NET 6 as they represent the new idiomatic way to bootstrap (configure and start) the relevant host for the majority of scenarios.

  • The CreateHostBuilder method would be removed as the patterns that were previously reliant on this are no longer necessary
  • The Startup class would be removed and its contents applied as applicable to the calls to WebApplicationBuilder in Program.cs

Asp net core issue: https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/issues/34123

item.title: Issue: Update FSharp repo to build with net 6.0 - @KevinRansom
item.description: Title says it all.

item.title: Issue: Automate FCS benchmarks - @TIHan
item.description:
Implement automation of FCS benchmarks

Effectively, we need to quantitatively measure the impact of our performance improvements.

item.title: Issue: July 2021 rough issue worklist - @KevinRansom
item.description:
And we're back with another month-ish of work. Here it is:

  • [ ] @vzarytovskii - #11532 Blocked - Waiting on fix to be released in .NET 6 SDK.

  • [ ] @vzarytovskii - #11803 Blocked - Waiting on feature to be released in project-system.

  • [ ] @TIHan - #11802

  • [ ] @kevinransom - #11800

  • [ ] @kevinransom - #11804

Note: 16.11 is a servicing-only release for us. We will not include any fixes unless they are high priority/regressions. All other feature work and bugs will be targeted for .NET 6/dev17.

item.title: Issue: `System.ObjectDisposedException : Cannot write to a closed TextWriter` in `printf` in numerous tests - @brettfo
item.description:
We've had several signed test runs fail recently with:

System.ObjectDisposedException : Cannot write to a closed TextWriter.

at System.IO.__Error.WriterClosed()
   at System.IO.StringWriter.Write(String value)
   at System.IO.TextWriter.SyncTextWriter.Write(String value)
   at Microsoft.FSharp.Core.PrintfImpl.clo@1264-14.Invoke(FSharpList`1 _args, FSharpFunc`2 initial) in D:\workspace\_work\1\s\src\fsharp\FSharp.Core\printf.fs:line 1266
   at Scripting.log[a](PrintfFormat`4 format) in D:\workspace\_work\1\s\src\scripts\scriptlib.fsx:line 81
   at TestFramework.logConfig(TestConfig cfg) in D:\workspace\_work\1\s\tests\FSharp.Test.Utilities\TestFramework.fs:line 360
   at TestFramework.initializeSuite() in D:\workspace\_work\1\s\tests\FSharp.Test.Utilities\TestFramework.fs:line 413
   at TestFramework.suiteHelpers@418.Invoke(Unit unitVar) in D:\workspace\_work\1\s\tests\FSharp.Test.Utilities\TestFramework.fs:line 418
   at System.Lazy`1.CreateValue()
--- End of stack trace from previous location where exception was thrown ---
   at System.Runtime.ExceptionServices.ExceptionDispatchInfo.Throw()
   at System.Lazy`1.get_Value()
   at TestFramework.testConfig(String testDir) in D:\workspace\_work\1\s\tests\FSharp.Test.Utilities\TestFramework.fs:line 436
   at SingleTest.singleTestBuildAndRun(String dir, Permutation p) in D:\workspace\_work\1\s\tests\fsharp\single-test.fs:line 375
   at FSharp.Tests.Core.singleTestBuildAndRun@35-1.Invoke(String dir, Permutation p) in D:\workspace\_work\1\s\tests\fsharp\tests.fs:line 35
   at FSharp.Tests.Core.GeneratedSignatureTests.innerpoly-GENERATED_SIGNATURE() in D:\workspace\_work\1\s\tests\fsharp\tests.fs:line 3131

There are usually ~500 failures, all with the same stack trace.

item.title: Issue: StackOverflow regression with Async.TaskAwait in a recursive loop in a FSharp.Core 5.0.2 - @Szer
item.description:
Repro steps

Provide the steps required to reproduce the problem:

open System
open System.Threading.Tasks

[<EntryPoint>]
let main _ =

    let rec loop (x: int) = async {
        do! Task.CompletedTask |> Async.AwaitTask
        Console.WriteLine x
        return! loop(x+1)
    }
    
    Async.RunSynchronously (loop 0)
    0

with

<PackageReference Update="FSharp.Core" Version="5.0.2"/>

dotnet run -c Release (both Release and Debug are affected)

Expected behavior

It should work

Actual behavior

Stack Overflow!

Known workarounds

  1. You could downgrade to 5.0.0 (Implicit in net5 SDK) or to 5.0.1 to make it work again:
<PackageReference Update="FSharp.Core" Version="5.0.0"/>
  1. OR Change Completed task to fake async with real continuation
do! Task.CompletedTask |> Async.AwaitTask

to

do! Task.Delay 1 |> Async.AwaitTask

Related information

Call stack shows that all COMPLETED tasks continuations started its trampoline on a stack of previous trampoline which causes SO.

It actually crashed production system which was working on Pulsar.Client.Dotnet (it currently using 5.0.2)

I also tried to find related changes with GitHub help: https://github.com/dotnet/fsharp/compare/5.0.0..5.0.2

But I couldn't find any related to FSharp.Core tags.

  • macOs
  • .NET5

UPDATED: 5.0.1 also works ok. So regression was introduced between 5.0.1 and 5.0.2

UPDATED2: suspect PR https://github.com/dotnet/fsharp/pull/11142

item.title: Issue: Address-of doesn't work when taking an immutable value for inref - @Happypig375
item.description:
Address-of doesn't work when taking an immutable value for inref

Repro steps

let f (x: inref<int>) = ()
let a = 1
f (&a)

error FS3230: A value defined in a module must be mutable in order to take its address, e.g. 'let mutable x = ...'

Expected behavior

Works.

Actual behavior

Errors.

Known workarounds

Making it mutable.

Related information

Provide any related information (optional):

  • Operating system: Windows
  • .NET Runtime kind (.NET Core, .NET Framework, Mono): SharpLab
  • Editing Tools (e.g. Visual Studio Version, Visual Studio): SharpLab

item.title: Issue: dotnet watch run broken on .NET 6 preview 5 - @2mol
item.description:
Repro steps

  1. mkdir bla && cd bla
  2. dotnet new console -lang F#
  3. dotnet run (just to confirm that it works)
  4. dotnet watch run

Expected behavior

The code compiles, runs, and the dotnet utility switches into "watch mode".

Actual behavior

The code compiles, runs, shows the "Hello world from F#" output, and then crashes with

watch : Waiting for a file to change before restarting dotnet...
Unhandled exception. System.InvalidOperationException: Cannot open project '/Users/user/bla/bla.fsproj' because the file extension '.fsproj' is not associated with a language.
   at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.MSBuild.DiagnosticReporter.Report(DiagnosticReportingMode mode, String message, Func`2 createException) in Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.Workspaces.MSBuild.dll:token 0x600003d+0x25
   at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.MSBuild.ProjectFileLoaderRegistry.TryGetLoaderFromProjectPath(String projectFilePath, DiagnosticReportingMode mode, IProjectFileLoader& loader) in Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.Workspaces.MSBuild.dll:token 0x6000103+0xd7
   at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.MSBuild.MSBuildProjectLoader.Worker.LoadProjectFileInfosAsync(String projectPath, DiagnosticReportingOptions reportingOptions, CancellationToken cancellationToken) in Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.Workspaces.MSBuild.dll:token 0x6000157+0x59
   at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.MSBuild.MSBuildProjectLoader.Worker.LoadProjectInfosFromPathAsync(String projectPath, DiagnosticReportingOptions reportingOptions, CancellationToken cancellationToken) in Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.Workspaces.MSBuild.dll:token 0x6000158+0xca
   at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.MSBuild.MSBuildProjectLoader.Worker.LoadAsync(CancellationToken cancellationToken) in Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.Workspaces.MSBuild.dll:token 0x6000156+0x13a
   at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.MSBuild.MSBuildProjectLoader.LoadProjectInfoAsync(String projectFilePath, ProjectMap projectMap, IProgress`1 progress, ILogger msbuildLogger, CancellationToken cancellationToken) in Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.Workspaces.MSBuild.dll:token 0x6000054+0x100
   at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.MSBuild.MSBuildWorkspace.OpenProjectAsync(String projectFilePath, ILogger msbuildLogger, IProgress`1 progress, CancellationToken cancellationToken) in Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.Workspaces.MSBuild.dll:token 0x6000067+0xb2
   at Microsoft.DotNet.Watcher.Tools.CompilationWorkspaceProvider.CreateProject(TaskCompletionSource`1 taskCompletionSource, String projectPath, IReporter reporter, CancellationToken cancellationToken) in dotnet-watch.dll:token 0x600017b+0xed
   at System.Threading.Tasks.Task.<>c.<ThrowAsync>b__140_1(Object state) in System.Private.CoreLib.dll:token 0x6002d46+0x0
   at System.Threading.QueueUserWorkItemCallbackDefaultContext.Execute() in System.Private.CoreLib.dll:token 0x6002a89+0x14
   at System.Threading.ThreadPoolWorkQueue.Dispatch() in System.Private.CoreLib.dll:token 0x6002a63+0xb2
   at System.Threading.PortableThreadPool.WorkerThread.WorkerThreadStart() in System.Private.CoreLib.dll:token 0x6002b3e+0x57
   at System.Threading.Thread.StartCallback() in System.Private.CoreLib.dll:token 0x60026de+0xe

Known workarounds

none

Related information

  • Operating system: macOS Big Sur, on an M1 processor
  • .NET Runtime: .NET 6 (preview)

item.title: Issue: Support Visual Studio Code Map - @xperiandri
item.description:
To understand complex solutions or debug complex code it is nice to be able to present it visually. Visual Studio has an excellent Code Map feature but dropping F# solution or files onto a Code Map surface has no effect.

Describe the solution you'd like

Full support of Code Map generation and debugging on Code Map.

Additional context

Tested in Visual Studio Enterprise 2019 16.10.3

item.title: Issue: Bitwise or on a char enum should succeed or error at compile time - @Happypig375
item.description:
Bitwise or on a char enum should succeed or error at compile time

Repro steps

Microsoft (R) F# Interactive version 11.4.2.0 for F# 5.0
Copyright (c) Microsoft Corporation. All Rights Reserved.

For help type #help;;

> type Enum = A = 'A' | B = 'B';;
type Enum =
  | A = 'A'
  | B = 'B'

> Enum.A ||| Enum.B;;
val it : Enum

> box (Enum.A ||| Enum.B);;
System.NotSupportedException: Dynamic invocation of op_Addition involving coercions is not supported.
   at Microsoft.FSharp.Core.LanguagePrimitives.BinaryOpDynamicImplTable`4.Invoke(String opName, T1 x, T2 y) in D:\workspace\_work\1\s\src\fsharp\FSharp.Core\prim-types.fs:line 2582
   at <StartupCode$FSI_0004>.$FSI_0004.main@()
Stopped due to error

Expected behavior

Either the operation succeeds or errors at compile time.

Actual behavior

It fails at run time.

Known workarounds None.

Related Information 
Microsoft Visual Studio Community 2019
Version 16.10.3
VisualStudio.16.Release/16.10.3+31424.327
Microsoft .NET Framework
Version 4.8.04084

Installed Version: Community

Visual C++ 2019   00435-60000-00000-AA759
Microsoft Visual C++ 2019

ADL Tools Service Provider   1.0
This package contains services used by Data Lake tools

ASA Service Provider   1.0

For additional information, visit https://www.asp.net/

This package contains the Data Lake integration nodes for Server Explorer.

Xamarin.Android Reference Assemblies and MSBuild support.
    Mono: b4a3858
    Java.Interop: xamarin/java.interop/d16-10@f39db25
    ProGuard: Guardsquare/proguard/v7.0.1@912d149
    SQLite: xamarin/sqlite/3.35.4@85460d3
    Xamarin.Android Tools: xamarin/xamarin-android-tools/d16-10@c5732a0


Xamarin.iOS and Xamarin.Mac SDK   14.20.0.24 (c4b89cddb)
Xamarin.iOS and Xamarin.Mac Reference Assemblies and MSBuild support.

item.title: Issue: Improve intelligence for computation expression - @albertwoo
item.description:
Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

I am trying to write a UI library and want to use computation expression for the DSL. But the intelligence for the CustomOperation is very bad because it offers you too much options and no filter for it. This can also benefit other CE style libraries like farm, Fshttp etc.

Describe the solution you'd like

Provide a filter or priorities for the CD's Custom Operation.

Describe alternatives you've considered ...

Additional context ...

item.title: Issue: Crash in VS for files containing large pattern matches - @Happypig375
item.description:
Repro steps

  1. Checkout https://github.com/Happypig375/fsharp/tree/f20da155c31c90df62a526493757d5f3ce13cc3e
  2. Open FSharp.Compiler.Service.sln
  3. Open src/fsharp/FSharp.Core/Linq.fs
  4. Wait for typechecking
  5. VS crashes

Expected behavior

VS does not crash and typecheck successfully

Actual behavior VS crashes before typechecking is done.

Known workarounds

https://github.com/dotnet/fsharp/pull/11681/commits/97a870837196a62ab4f62aa1df8da77ba8962bc1 seems to have solved it.

Related information 
Microsoft Visual Studio Community 2019
Version 16.10.3
VisualStudio.16.Release/16.10.3+31424.327
Microsoft .NET Framework
Version 4.8.04084

Installed Version: Community

item.title: Issue: FormattableString interpolated string mishandles escaped brackets - @chkn
item.description:
When creating a FormattableString from an interpolated string, escaped brackets ({{ and }}) are not correctly handled, resulting in an invalid format string. See the comparison with C# below:

Repro steps

$ csi
Microsoft (R) Visual C# Interactive Compiler version 3.9.0-6.21124.20 ()
Copyright (C) Microsoft Corporation. All rights reserved.

Type "#help" for more information.
> FormattableString str = $"{{Hello}} world";
> str.Format
"{{Hello}} world"

$ dotnet fsi

Microsoft (R) F# Interactive version 11.4.2.0 for F# 5.0
Copyright (c) Microsoft Corporation. All Rights Reserved.

For help type #help;;

> let str = $"{{Hello}} world" : System.FormattableString;;
val str : System.FormattableString =
  <ToString exception: Input string was not in a correct format.>

> str.Format;;
val it : string = "{Hello} world"

Expected behavior

The FormattableString created by F# should match the one created by C# for the same interpolated string.

Actual behavior

Escaped brackets are unescaped into the format string, causing it to be invalid.

Known workarounds

Modify the interpolated string or call FormattableStringFactory.Create directly.

Related information

Provide any related information (optional):

  • macOS 10.15.7
  • .NET Runtime kind (.NET Core, .NET Framework, Mono): mono 6.12.0.140 (2020-02/51d876a041e, .NET Core 5.0.301
  • Editing Tools (e.g. Visual Studio Version, Visual Studio)

item.title: Issue: Implicit conversion to .NET delegates broken on spliced quotations - @blaulaub
item.description:
Repro steps

In F# REPL, compare the direct quotation

<@ System.Func<int,int>( fun x -> x+1 ) @>

to the spliced quotation

<@ fun x -> x+1 @> |> fun e -> <@ System.Func<int,int>( %e ) @>

Expected behavior

Both should produce a .NET delegate (Expr.NewDelegate).

Actual behavior

The spliced quotation produces a lambda (Expr.Lambda)

Known workarounds

My current workaround is not to use splicing but to do it programmatically:

<@ fun x -> x+1 @>
|> function
| Patterns.Lambda(v, body) -> Expr.NewDelegate(typeof<System.Func<int,int>>, [v], body)
| _ as other -> failwith (sprintf "unsupported case %A" other)
|> fun e -> Expr.Cast<System.Func<int, int>>(e)

Related information

  • first raised on StackOverflow https://stackoverflow.com/questions/68228386/why-does-direct-code-quotation-of-system-func-yield-a-delegate-and-spliced-quota
  • Windows 10, .NET Core 5

item.title: Issue: [dev17] Microsoft.FSharp.ShimHelpers.props isn't deployed in VS installer. - @brettfo
Issue: [dev17] Semantic coloring doesn't work in latest dev17.0 preview - @brettfo
item.description: The identifier EntryPoint should be teal and function identifiers (e.g., from, sprintf, etc.) should be light yellow.

item.title: Issue: In-memory cross-project reference not working for project using a generative type provider - @jkone27
item.description:
When I use VS Build has 0 errors, but VS ide says there is errors.. this repo should allow to reproduce https://github.com/jkone27/test-broken

Workaround

Tools -> Options -> Text Editor -> F# -> Performance -> Turn off "Enable in-memory cross project references"

Details

I am on win10 and vs2019 (and vs2020 also affected)

e..g in another repo

item.title: Issue: FsiEvaluationSession cant find Fsharp.Core when hosted in another process. - @goswinr
item.description:
Problem

The constructor of FsiEvaluationSession cant find Fsharp.Core when FCS is hosted in another process. I used v40.0 but this problem probably existed since FCS 34.0. see https://github.com/dotnet/fsharp/issues/9064 When FCS is hosted in another app ( Rhino3d in my case) the directory at Directory.GetCurrentDirectory() probably does not contain FSharp.Core.dll. A non-descriptive StopProcessingExn is raised.

The useful information about the problem is not in the exception, but only in the FSI error stream:

error FS0078: Unable to find the file 'FSharp.Core.dll' in any of
 C:\Windows\Microsoft.NET\Framework64\v4.0.30319
 C:\Windows\Microsoft.NET\Framework64\v4.0.30319\WPF
 C:\Program Files\Rhino 7\System
 C:\Program Files\Rhino 7\System\

Workaround

The workaround is to set the current directory to the location of FSharp.Core.dll before calling FsiEvaluationSession.Create(...) for example via: Directory.SetCurrentDirectory(Path.GetDirectoryName(Reflection.Assembly.GetAssembly([].GetType()).Location)) It took me one year, two other issues on this repo, and the reading of the now more descriptive FSI error stream (since 40.0) in addition to the exception, to understand what the problem was and find this workaround.

item.title: Issue: Go to Metadata for extension methods displays extended class or interface instead of extension class - @xperiandri
item.description:
If I open Go to definition on extension method the class or interface of that extension method appears instead of extension class

Visual Studio 2019 16.10.3 F# tools 16.10.0-beta21262.7

item.title: Issue: Inheriting IComparable should satisfy a comparison constraint - @kentcb
item.description:
The following does not compile:

type Wrapper<'T when 'T :> IComparable> = {
    Values: Set<'T>
}

It insists on a comparison constraint on 'T even though the documentation (and intuition) suggests that inheriting from IComparable is the same thing:

'T must implement IComparable, or be an array type with comparable elements, or be System.IntPtr or System.UIntPtr. Also, 'T must not have the NoComparison attribute.

It doesn't matter whether the constraint is for IComparable, IComparable<'T>, IStructuralComparable, or all of them.

Expected behavior

The code should compile.

Actual behavior

A type parameter is missing a constraint 'when 'T : comparison'

Known workarounds

  • Add an additional constraint. But note that this is exactly what I'm trying to avoid. I have my own interface which is intended to amalgamate all type requirements, including the need for comparison. By adding a single constraint of inheriting from that interface, the compiler should know it supports comparison.
  • Don't use Map<'T> and use HashSet<'T> instead. This isn't really an acceptable workaround in my case, since it introduces mutability.
